Performing initial audit of PKGBUILD to ensure that we can safely run `makepkg`
LLM auditresponse for PKGBUILD:
<security_assessment>
<decision>SAFE</decision>
<details>
The PKGBUILD defines standard sources: a local script `${pkgname}.sh` included in the package, and architecture-specific tarballs downloaded from Sublime Text's official domain (download.sublimetext.com). The URLs are constructed using `pkgver` and architecture variables, but point to expected, legitimate locations. No command substitution or dynamic code execution is present in the `source` array. The top-level scope contains only standard variable assignments (pkgname, pkgver, etc.), with no inline scripts, function calls, or side-effecting expressions. The `pkgver()` function is absent, so no version-fetch logic runs during sourcing. Checksums are explicitly defined and not set to SKIP, even for binary sources. No obfuscated code, network requests outside the declared sources, or data exfiltration mechanisms are present in the global scope. Running `makepkg --nobuild --nodeps --noprepare` will safely fetch the source tarballs and the local script without executing any potentially harmful commands.
</details>
<summary>
Safe source fetching with no malicious side effects
</summary>
</security_assessment>

LLM auditresponse for sublime-text-dev.install:
<security_assessment>
<decision>SAFE</decision>
<details>
The `sublime-text-dev.install` file contains two standard AUR install script functions: `post_install()` and `post_upgrade()`. Both functions use a here-document (`<< _EOF`) to display informational messages to the user after installation or upgrade. The messages provide legitimate usage notes, including the fact that the dev branch requires a license, recommendations for stable alternatives, and a tip to disable update reminders by setting `"update_check": false` in the user's preferences.

There are no network operations, file modifications, system calls, or executable actions performed. The content is purely informational and uses only safe shell constructs (cat with a heredoc). No obfuscation, unexpected commands, or potentially harmful operations are present. This is typical and safe behavior for an AUR install script.
</details>
<summary>
Purely informational; no harmful actions
</summary>
</security_assessment>

[1/3] Reviewing PKGBUILD, sublime-text-dev.sh...

LLM auditresponse for sublime-text-dev.sh:
<security_assessment>
<decision>SAFE</decision>
<details>
The script `sublime-text-dev.sh` is a simple wrapper that executes the Sublime Text binary located at `@ST_PATH@/sublime_text` with all received arguments passed through via `"$@"`. The use of `exec` ensures the process replaces the shell script directly, which is a standard and safe practice. The path `@ST_PATH@` is a placeholder, typically substituted during the package build process to point to the actual installation directory (e.g., `/opt/sublime-text`). There is no obfuscation, network activity, unexpected file manipulation, or execution of downloaded code. The script performs a minimal and expected role: launching the application.

This behavior is consistent with standard packaging practices for desktop applications distributed via AUR, especially when managing binary packages that require wrapper scripts for path resolution or desktop integration. The script does not introduce any security risks on its own, as it does not manipulate system settings, exfiltrate data, or run additional commands beyond launching the declared binary. While the `@ST_PATH@` placeholder must be correctly resolved during packaging, that substitution is handled safely by the build system and not within this runtime script.

No malicious or suspicious behavior is present. The script is simple, transparent, and does exactly what a launcher script should do.
</details>
<summary>
Simple, safe launcher script with no malicious behavior
</summary>
</security_assessment>

[2/3] Reviewing PKGBUILD...
+ Reviewed sublime-text-dev.sh. Status: SAFE -- Simple, safe launcher script with no malicious behavior
LLM auditresponse for PKGBUILD:
<security_assessment>
<decision>SAFE</decision>
<details>
The PKGBUILD is for the Sublime Text developer build and follows standard AUR packaging practices. It downloads precompiled binaries directly from the official Sublime Text website (`download.sublimetext.com`), which is the expected and legitimate source. The source URLs are properly formatted, and separate checksums (sha512sums) are provided for both x86_64 and aarch64 architectures, ensuring integrity.

The prepare() and package() functions perform routine operations: patching paths in a wrapper script and desktop file, installing icons via symlinks, and setting up application menu entries. There is no use of dangerous commands like `eval`, `curl|bash`, or downloading/execution of remote scripts. The modifications are transparent and confined to rebranding paths from `/opt/sublime_text` to use `/usr/bin/subl`, consistent with standard packaging hygiene.

No suspicious network activity, obfuscated code, or unauthorized system modifications are present. The package conflicts with the stable `sublime-text` version and provides `sublime-text`, which is appropriate. All actions serve the purpose of correctly integrating the upstream binary into the system.
</details>
<summary>
Standard AUR packaging with official source and no malicious content</summary>
</security_assessment>

LLM auditresponse for __main__.py:
<security_assessment>
<decision>SAFE</decision>
<details>
The provided Python script is a simple command-line tool that uses the `certifi` package to either print the path to the CA certificate bundle (`where()`) or the contents of the bundle (`contents()`). The script uses standard argparse functionality to handle command-line arguments and exposes functionality already present in the certifi library.

There is no network communication, file modification, code execution, or data exfiltration. The operations are limited to reading a bundled file (certificates) and printing either its path or contents to stdout. This behavior is consistent with the intended use of the certifi package and poses no security risk.

The code is transparent, minimal, and does not employ obfuscation, dynamic code evaluation, or dangerous system calls. It does not modify system state or interact with sensitive user data beyond what is expected from a certificate management utility.
</details>
<summary>
Safe: No malicious behavior detected</summary>
</security_assessment>

LLM auditresponse for core.py:
<security_assessment>
<decision>SAFE</decision>
<details>
The provided `core.py` file is part of the `certifi` Python package, which supplies a curated collection of trusted CA certificates (in `cacert.pem`) for use in SSL/TLS verification. The code conditionally implements different strategies for accessing this file based on the Python version, using modern `importlib.resources` for Python 3.7+ and falling back to file system path manipulation for older versions.

All operations are confined to reading and exposing the location of the bundled certificate file. The logic uses standard library patterns for resource access and does not perform network requests, execute external code, or access sensitive system data. The global caching of `_CACERT_CTX` and `_CACERT_PATH` is an optimization to avoid repeated file system operations and is thread-safe within the described constraints.

There is no obfuscation, unexpected behavior, or supply-chain attack vector. The code aligns with standard packaging practices and the expected functionality of the `certifi` package.
</details>
<summary>
Standard certificate access logic, no malicious behavior</summary>
</security_assessment>

<security_assessment>
<decision>SAFE</decision>
<details>
The provided file `imp.py` is a simple compatibility module that re-exports functionality from Python's `importlib` module to mimic the now-removed `imp` module. It imports `reload`, `cache_from_source`, and `source_from_cache` from `importlib` and includes them in `__all__` for public exposure. There are no network operations, file system modifications, obfuscated code, or dangerous system calls. The code serves a legitimate compatibility purpose and does not execute any logic beyond re-exporting safe, built-in functions. It does not introduce any malicious behavior or supply-chain risks.

No suspicious or extraneous operations are present. The file aligns with common practices for supporting legacy imports in Python codebases.
</details>

LLM auditresponse for sublime_plugin.py:
<security_assessment>
<decision>SAFE</decision>
<details>
The provided Python file, `sublime_plugin.py`, is a core module for the Sublime Text plugin system. It defines APIs for plugin loading, event handling, command registration, and resource management within the Sublime Text editor environment. The code is consistent with standard plugin architecture patterns, handling class registration, lifecycle callbacks (e.g., `plugin_loaded`, `plugin_unloaded`), and event dispatching.

There are no signs of malicious behavior such as network exfiltration, code obfuscation, or execution of external scripts. The file imports standard modules and Sublime Text's own APIs (`sublime`, `sublime_api`). It uses `importlib` for plugin module reloading, which is normal behavior in editor plugin systems. The use of `certifi` to set `SSL_CERT_FILE` is a common practice for ensuring SSL certificate trust in embedded Python environments and does not represent a security threat. All functionality serves the expected purpose of enabling and managing editor extensions.

The code contains no calls to `curl`, `wget`, `eval`, `exec`, or other dangerous operations. It is well-structured, extensively commented, and follows Python best practices. The file appears to be part of the legitimate Sublime Text plugin infrastructure.
</details>
<summary>
Standard Sublime Text plugin infrastructure, no malicious code found
</summary>
</security_assessment>

LLM auditresponse for py.typed:
<security_assessment>
<decision>SAFE</decision>
<details>
The file `py.typed` is an empty file commonly used in Python packages to indicate that the package includes type annotations and is compliant with PEP 561. This allows type checkers like `mypy` to recognize the package as typed. The absence of content in this file is not unusual or suspicious; it serves a purely declarative purpose. There is no executable code, no network activity, and no system modification possible from an empty `py.typed` file.

Since the file contains no code or data, it cannot be used to exfiltrate information, download payloads, or execute malicious logic. Its presence is a standard practice in Python packaging and type checking workflows. Therefore, this file poses no security risk.

This is consistent with expected packaging behavior for Python libraries and presents no evidence of tampering or injected malicious content.
</details>
<summary>
Empty py.typed file is standard and safe
</summary>
</security_assessment>

LLM auditresponse for sublime_text.desktop:
<security_assessment>
<decision>SAFE</decision>
<details>
The file is a standard desktop entry (.desktop) used to integrate Sublime Text with the system's application menu and desktop environment. It declares the application name, icon, categories, MIME type, and custom actions (new window, new file) using well-known desktop entry specifications. All fields point to local resources within /opt/sublime_text/, and there are no network operations, file manipulations, or executable commands outside of launching the application.

The Exec lines invoke the Sublime Text binary with predefined arguments, a common and legitimate practice in .desktop files. The OnlyShowIn=Unity; entries are harmless compatibility hints targeting the Unity desktop environment. There is no evidence of obfuscation, code injection, or malicious behavior. The file adheres to standard packaging practices and poses no supply-chain risk.

No suspicious or dangerous constructs such as shell commands, script invocations, or remote data transfers are present. The file serves purely a declarative purpose for desktop integration. While Sublime Text itself includes telemetry features (as noted in upstream documentation), this .desktop file does not enable or enhance such behavior. This constitutes expected upstream functionality, not a compromise.
</details>
<summary>
Standard desktop entry; no malicious content</summary>
</security_assessment>

LLM auditresponse for cacert.pem:
<security_assessment>
<decision>SAFE</decision>
<details>
The provided file is a standard `cacert.pem` file, which contains a list of trusted X.509 root certificates in PEM format. Each entry includes certificate details such as issuer, subject, serial number, fingerprints, and the Base64-encoded certificate data between `-----BEGIN CERTIFICATE-----` and `-----END CERTIFICATE-----` markers. 

The content consists solely of static certificate data used for establishing trust in SSL/TLS connections. There are no executable statements, scripts, network calls, file manipulations, or any form of dynamic code. The presence of standard root CAs like DigiCert, GlobalSign, Google Trust Services, and others is expected and legitimate.

No suspicious commands (e.g., `curl`, `wget`, `eval`, `base64` decoding of payloads) appear in the file. The structure and content align exactly with what would be expected in a CA bundle used by operating systems or applications for certificate validation.

While truncated, the head and tail of the file show consistent certificate entries without any injected code or obfuscation. The omission of 3000+ lines of boilerplate certificate data is typical for such files and does not raise concern.
</details>
<summary>
Trusted root certificate bundle, no executable code
</summary>
</security_assessment>

The provided `sublime.py` file is a Python API module for Sublime Text, defining classes, enums, and functions that expose editor functionality to plugins. All functions interact safely with the Sublime Text API (via `sublime_api`), and standard library usage is limited to harmless operations like JSON serialization and HTML escaping.

The file is well-documented, consistent with Sublime Text's public plugin API, and exhibits no obfuscation or anomalous behavior. Suspicious patterns such as "command_url" or "output:output" in the truncated section refer to internal UI element identifiers, not network activity. References to callbacks, dialogs, and input handling are part of normal editor functionality.

This is a legitimate API shim for plugin development, not a supply-chain trojan or malicious wrapper.

Performing initial audit of PKGBUILD to ensure that we can safely run `makepkg`
LLM auditresponse for PKGBUILD:
<security_assessment>
<decision>SAFE</decision>
<details>
This PKGBUILD is a standard AUR package for Sublime Text Dev. The analysis focuses only on code that executes during `makepkg --nobuild --nodeps --noprepare` (sourcing the PKGBUILD and fetching the `source` array). No top-level command substitutions, `pkgver()` functions, or other dynamic execution exist. The `source` array includes a local helper script (`${pkgname}.sh`) and architecture‑specific tarballs from the official `download.sublimetext.com` domain. All URLs point to the package’s own upstream. Checksums are provided and are not `SKIP` for the tarballs. The `prepare()` and `package()` functions are not executed during this initial step and will be reviewed separately. No evidence of malicious downloading, data exfiltration, or obfuscated code is present in the portion that runs now.
</details>
<summary>
Standard PKGBUILD with official upstream sources; no runtime danger.</summary>
</security_assessment>

+ Initial PKGBUILD audit complete [grey50]-- Standard PKGBUILD with official upstream sources; no runtime danger.[/]

LLM auditresponse for PKGBUILD:
<security_assessment>
<decision>SAFE</decision>
<details>
The PKGBUILD performs standard packaging tasks: downloading the official Sublime Text dev build from `download.sublimetext.com`, verifying integrity via hardcoded SHA-512 checksums, installing the binary to `/opt/sublime_text`, creating desktop icons and a launcher script (`subl`) with proper `sed` path substitutions. No malicious network requests, obfuscated code, or suspicious operations are present. All sources are pinned with checksums, and the build/install steps are conventional for an AUR package.
</details>
<summary>Standard AUR PKGBUILD, no malicious content.</summary>
</security_assessment>

The content is purely cosmetic, reminding users that a license is required and how to disable update checks. No malicious or suspicious behavior is present.
</details>
<summary>Only informational output, no malicious actions.</summary>

LLM auditresponse for sublime_text.desktop:
<security_assessment>
<decision>SAFE</decision>
<details>
This is a standard `.desktop` file for the Sublime Text editor. It defines the application name, icon, executable path (`/opt/sublime_text/sublime_text`), MIME types, categories, and desktop actions. No obfuscation, network requests, dangerous commands, or system modifications are present. The file conforms to the freedesktop.org Desktop Entry Specification and is a normal packaging artifact. There is no evidence of any supply-chain attack or malicious behavior.
</details>
<summary>Standard desktop entry file; no security issues.</summary>
</security_assessment>

<security_assessment>
<decision>SAFE</decision>
<details>
The file `sublime_types.py` contains only Python type alias definitions using the `type` keyword, with an import of `KindId` and `CompletionItem` from the `sublime` module. It performs no executable code, no network requests, no file operations, no system modifications, and no obfuscation. The content is purely declarative type annotations used for Sublime Text plugin development, which is consistent with the purpose of the package. There are no indicators of malicious behavior.

LLM auditresponse for __main__.py:
<security_assessment>
<decision>SAFE</decision>
<details>
The file is a simple CLI entry point for the `certifi` package, which provides Mozilla's CA Bundle. It imports `contents` and `where` from `certifi` and prints either the certificate contents or the file path based on a command-line argument. There are no network requests, obfuscated code, dangerous system calls, or any behavior that deviates from the expected functionality of providing certificate bundle information. This is standard packaging practice for a Python package entry point.
</details>
<summary>Benign certifi CLI entry point, no security issues.</summary>
</security_assessment>

+ Reviewed src/sublime_text/Lib/python3/certifi/__main__.py. Status: SAFE -- Benign certifi CLI entry point, no security issues.
LLM auditresponse for core.py:
<security_assessment>
<decision>SAFE</decision>
<details>
The file `core.py` is a standard implementation of the certifi module, which locates the bundled CA certificate file (`cacert.pem`). It uses Python's `importlib.resources` to retrieve the path (or falls back to `os.path.dirname(__file__)` for older versions). There are no suspicious network requests, obfuscated code, dangerous commands (`eval`, `curl`, `wget`, etc.), or unexpected system modifications. The code is entirely inline with its stated purpose and does not exhibit any characteristics of a supply chain attack. The only minor discrepancy is the docstring referring to `certifi.py` while the file is named `core.py`, but this is a harmless naming detail.
</details>
<summary>Standard certifi module, no malicious behavior found.</summary>
</security_assessment>

LLM auditresponse for cacert.pem:
<security_assessment>
<decision>SAFE</decision>
<details>
The file is a standard CA certificate bundle (`cacert.pem`) containing PEM-encoded X.509 root certificates with metadata comments (issuer, subject, fingerprints). All observed certificates belong to well-known Certificate Authorities (GlobalSign, Entrust, DigiCert, Google Trust Services, etc.) and their contents match legitimate, publicly-distributed root certificates. There are no executable instructions, obfuscated code, network requests, or file operations. The file is static data used for SSL/TLS certificate validation – a routine and expected component in any application that makes secure HTTP connections, including Sublime Text. No evidence of tampering, injected certificates, or malicious intent was found. The presence of this file in an AUR package is standard practice.
</details>
<summary>Standard CA certificate bundle, no malicious content.</summary>
</security_assessment>

[7/10] Reviewing src/sublime_text/Lib/python314/sublime.py, src/sublime_text/Lib/python314/sublime_plugin.py, src/sublime_text/Lib/python3/certifi/__init__.py...
+ Reviewed src/sublime_text/Lib/python3/certifi/cacert.pem. Status: SAFE -- Standard CA certificate bundle, no malicious content.
LLM auditresponse for sublime_plugin.py:
<security_assessment>
<decision>SAFE</decision>
<details>
The file `sublime_plugin.py` is a standard component of Sublime Text's plugin system. It handles plugin loading, event dispatching, and provides base classes for plugins. The code is well-structured and contains no suspicious patterns such as network requests (curl, wget), obfuscated code (eval, base64), or unexpected system modifications. The only external interactions are standard Python imports and calls to Sublime Text's own API (`sublime_api`). The custom import finder (`PackagePathFinder`) loads plugins from compressed packages within Sublime Text's own resource system, which is expected behavior. The final section sets up site-packages and SSL certificate file paths, which is normal for a Python application. There is no evidence of malicious or dangerous behavior.
</details>
<summary>Standard Sublime Text plugin system file, no security issues.</summary>
</security_assessment>

[8/10] Reviewing src/sublime_text/Lib/python314/sublime.py, src/sublime_text/Lib/python3/certifi/__init__.py...
+ Reviewed src/sublime_text/Lib/python314/sublime_plugin.py. Status: SAFE -- Standard Sublime Text plugin system file, no security issues.
LLM auditresponse for __init__.py:
<security_assessment>
<decision>SAFE</decision>
<details>
This file is a standard Python package initialization file. It simply imports `contents` and `where` from a sibling module `core`, defines `__all__` for the public API, and sets a version string. There are no network requests, no dangerous commands (e.g., `eval`, `curl`, `base64`), no obfuscation, and no file operations that could indicate malicious behavior. It is a benign, minimal file consistent with normal Python packaging practices.
</details>
<summary>Simple Python init file, no security issues.</summary>
</security_assessment>

[9/10] Reviewing src/sublime_text/Lib/python314/sublime.py...
+ Reviewed src/sublime_text/Lib/python3/certifi/__init__.py. Status: SAFE -- Simple Python init file, no security issues.
LLM auditresponse for sublime.py:
<security_assessment>
<decision>SAFE</decision>
<details>
The file `sublime.py` is the standard Python API module for Sublime Text plugins. It contains only class definitions, enumerations, and utility functions that interact with the native `sublime_api` module. There is no obfuscated code, no network requests (no `curl`, `wget`, `eval`, `base64`, or `exec`), and no operations that exfiltrate data or download/execute external content. The only file I/O is `open(executable_path(), 'rb')` in `executable_hash()`, which reads the editor's own binary to compute an MD5 hash — this is normal upstream functionality for installation identification, not a supply-chain attack. The file is consistent with standard packaging of the Sublime Text editor and does not exhibit any genuinely malicious behavior.
</details>
<summary>Standard Sublime Text API module, no malicious code found.</summary>
</security_assessment>
